tagline: Stop writing code. Start steering intent.

slides.heading: The Quote

stat.value: The hottest new programming language is English.
stat.label: Andrej Karpathy, AI researcher and founder of Eureka Labs

slides.callout: The question is no longer IF you use AI agents. It's HOW well you direct them.

slides.heading: The Vibe Coding Revolution
slides.subheading: Why are you still writing code line by line?
points: Is it cost? Or Trust? Or just inertia? The era of manual coding is over — the question is how fast you can adapt.
The shift is from 'writing lines' to 'steering intent' — you are the pilot, not the engine.
This isn't about replacing developers — it's about 10x-ing what one developer can ship.

stat.label: productivity gains for AI-native development teams

slides.callout: The question is no longer IF you use AI agents. It's HOW well you direct them.

slides.heading: The Agent is the Engine
slides.subheading: Understand the metaphor — or crash at high speed.
points: The AI Agent is a V12 engine: raw power, speed, and torque — but no steering wheel.
The Developer is the Driver: you provide direction, judgment, and braking instincts.
Logic is the chassis: without it, the engine (AI) will twist the frame and destroy itself.
A faster engine only makes a bad driver crash sooner — skill still matters, it just changed.
Your new core skill: prompt architecture, context management, and output verification.
slides.callout: More power with less control is not progress. Master the wheel before flooring it.

slides.heading: The State of AI Adoption
slides.subheading: The gap between adopters and resisters is compounding daily.
points: AI-native teams are shipping features in hours that previously took weeks.
Agentic Workflows are the 2026 standard — not an experiment, not a trend.
Companies using coding agents report measurable acceleration across every stage of delivery.
The divide: developers who leverage AI as a multiplier vs. those who compete against it.

stat.label: faster pull request cycle — from 9.6 days down to 2.4 days

slides.callout: This is not a wave coming. It already arrived. The question is whether you're surfing or drowning.

slides.heading: Choosing Your Fuel
slides.subheading: Model selection is economic engineering, not brand loyalty.
points: High-Reasoning Models (Opus, Pro, o3): Architecting, debugging deep logic, security audits.
High-Speed Models (Flash, Haiku, Sonnet): Boilerplate, CRUD, repetitive pattern generation.
Cost vs. Quality: Using a $15/M token model for a 3-line function is wasteful engineering.
Cascade strategy: Planner model (expensive) → Executor model (cheap) → Reviewer (medium).
Always benchmark: run both models on the same task and measure output quality vs. cost ratio.
slides.callout: The right model for the right task. Economic engineering is a developer skill now.

slides.heading: Everyone is using AI Agents
slides.subheading: The GAP in hype vs. reality is closing fast — don't be the last to adopt.
slides.callout: You are not behind the curve. The curve is behind you. The question is how fast you can catch up.

slides.heading: The Architecture of Intent
slides.subheading: Markdown is now the universal language for agent instruction.
points: Agents: Define the persona, expertise level, and decision-making authority of the AI.
Skills: Map exactly what the agent can execute — tools, APIs, file access, and actions.
Rules: Hard constraints that cannot be overridden — linting rules, framework locks, style guides.
Memory: What persists across context resets — summaries, decision logs, project state.
Compose these four layers and you have a fully governed, reproducible AI collaborator.
slides.callout: A well-written AGENTS.md is worth more than 1,000 ad-hoc prompts.

slides.heading: Context is Everything
slides.subheading: Context is your agent's short-term memory — treat it like gold.
points: The context window is finite and precious — every token of noise reduces signal quality.
Attach files strategically: more context is NOT always better context — be surgical.
Context Window Poisoning: circular errors, verbose logs, and dead code kill AI reasoning.
Integrity Checks: always validate what the AI 'remembers' before triggering execution.
When context degrades, the AI starts hallucinating confidently — the worst failure mode.
slides.callout: Garbage in, garbage out — but at AI speed. Poisoned context produces confident nonsense.

slides.heading: Ask vs. Act vs. Plan
slides.subheading: Strategic workflow design separates pilots from passengers.
points: For any complex task: always demand a written Markdown plan BEFORE execution starts.
The Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) checkpoint: never skip the plan review step.
Avoiding the Refactoring Rampage: scope agents tightly — never give full project access.
Ask Mode: explore options, get alternatives, understand tradeoffs — no side effects.
Plan Mode: generate a step-by-step execution strategy with rollback points defined.
Act Mode: execute only after the plan has been reviewed and explicitly approved by you.
slides.callout: Never let an AI touch the whole codebase at once. Scope. Review. Execute. Verify.

slides.heading: MCPs vs. CLIs
slides.subheading: The plumbing of the agentic era — know what carries your water.
points: CLIs are the past: single-turn, stateless, manually triggered — not built for agent loops.
Model Context Protocol (MCP) is the future: persistent, composable, agent-native interfaces.
Context7: real-time, version-specific documentation so your agent stops hallucinating APIs.
The Challenge: permissioning and state drift compound in long-running agentic loops.
Best practice: scope MCP permissions tightly — agents should have least-privilege access.
slides.callout: An agent with MCP access to your production database and no guardrails is a liability.

slides.heading: Full-Stack Vibe
slides.subheading: Structure your project so agents never lose context crossing the stack.
points: Single-project monorepos: client and server in one view keeps agent context coherent.
Next.js and TanStack Start: unified context for both sides — no mental context switch.
Monorepo strategy: scope your agent to a specific package using workspace filters.
Shared types: define your data contracts once and let agents propagate them automatically.
Naming conventions are load-bearing: consistent names help AI infer intent without instructions.
slides.callout: Folder structure is agent communication. How you organize files tells the AI what matters.

slides.heading: Guardrails & Automated Safety
slides.subheading: The fastest code is useless if it breaks in production.
points: Pre-tool Hooks: auto-formatting, ESLint, and test runners as mandatory entry gates.
Code Review Agents: use a second AI model to audit the first — two models, one reviewer.
Agent-ref-id: tag AI-generated vs human-written code with lovable-tagger for auditability.
Semantic diff checks: before committing, verify the change scope matches the intended spec.
The meta-rule: if an agent can't pass your CI pipeline, it doesn't get to merge. Ever.
slides.callout: Trust but verify — preferably with another AI doing the verification automatically.

slides.heading: Test-Driven Vibe Coding
slides.subheading: Generation speed without verification speed is just fast failure.
points: The pace of AI generation demands an equal pace of automated verification.
Workflow: Write Failing Test → AI Generation → Green Light → Commit. Never skip steps.
Testing is MORE important now than in the manual era — agents don't feel shame about bugs.
Property-based testing: generate hundreds of edge cases and let the AI verify against all.
TDVC rule: if there is no test for it, the AI has no constraint on how it implements it.
slides.callout: An AI that writes code without a test spec is an agent with no rules. Write the test first.

slides.heading: Pruning & Knowledge Compression
slides.subheading: Manage context like a garden — prune regularly or it grows wild.
points: Context Windows: use compact formats (SMOL, minified JSON) to preserve token space.
Pruning: periodically summarize the conversation and start a fresh session with the digest.
Dual Documentation: READMEs written for humans, .md summaries formatted for agent parsing.
Session checkpointing: save the agent's understanding as structured state before reset.
The anti-pattern: never let context grow so stale that the AI contradicts its earlier output.
slides.callout: A fresh context with a great summary outperforms a bloated context with the full history.

slides.heading: The Brainstorm Phase
slides.subheading: Before a single line of code — have the conversation.
points: Start with a high-level problem statement: what does this product need to DO for users?
Brainstorm unique Skills: what capabilities differentiate this product from generic alternatives?
Identify edge cases before any code exists — it costs zero tokens to think them through now.
Use the AI as a sparring partner: challenge your assumptions before they become architecture.
Output artifact: a structured Product Brief that becomes your agent's primary context document.
slides.callout: Thirty minutes of structured brainstorming with an AI saves thirty hours of refactoring later.

slides.heading: Architecture & Stack Selection
slides.subheading: Choose a stack that your agents can navigate without getting lost.
points: AI-friendly stacks: Next.js App Router, TanStack Start, and Remix dominate the vibe era.
Choosing the right DNA: SPA for dashboards, RSC for content, serverless for APIs.
React Native and Flutter: mobile stacks with strong agent tooling and predictable patterns.
Avoid exotic stacks: the more niche your framework, the worse your AI's context about it.
Document your architecture decisions in ADR files — agents and humans both benefit.
slides.callout: The best stack for AI development is the one with the most training data behind it.

slides.heading: Git & Branching for Agents
slides.subheading: Version control is the safety net that makes agentic development safe.
points: Branch-per-feature strategy: isolate agent context to a single concern per branch.
Commit message templates: structured messages that describe WHAT changed and WHY.
Use PRs as the primary review interface — this is where HITL happens at the team level.
Atomic commits: each agent execution should produce one verifiable, reversible commit.
The hard rule: never let an agent commit directly to main. Always go through PR review.
slides.callout: Git is the time machine that lets you undo what the agent broke at 3am on a Friday.

slides.heading: The Council of Experts
slides.subheading: Define specialized agent personas — generalists produce mediocre output.
points: Frontend Engineer: component architecture, UI state, accessibility, and design systems.
Backend Engineer: API contract design, database optimization, and performance profiling.
DevOps & Security: CI/CD pipelines, secrets management, cloud hardening, cost optimization.
QA Specialist: automated test-case generation, edge-case hunting, and regression detection.
Rule: always route tasks to the most specialized agent available — avoid using a generalist.
slides.callout: A frontend agent should never touch your database schema. Specialization creates accountability.

slides.heading: The Standard Six Foundation
slides.subheading: Every serious project needs these before the first feature is built.
points: Auth: authentication and authorization — identity is the first security layer.
Logging: structured, searchable, and correlated — you can't debug what you can't see.
Monitoring: real-time alerting, dashboards, and error tracking — Sentry, Datadog, or similar.
Feature Flags: deploy without releasing — decouple deployment from activation.
DB Migrations: versioned, reversible, and peer-reviewed before execution.
API Docs: OpenAPI spec generated and maintained automatically, not manually.
slides.callout: Use scaffolding agents to build this entire foundation in under 30 minutes.

slides.heading: Worktree: Parallel AI Operations
slides.subheading: Why run one agent when you can run five simultaneously?
points: Git worktrees allow multiple agents to work on separate features in true parallel.
Each worktree is an isolated working directory — agents cannot interfere with each other.
Managing state drift: schedule regular merge-back cycles to keep branches coherent.
Orchestrator pattern: one top-level agent coordinates multiple specialized worker agents.
Practical limit: 3–5 parallel agents is the sweet spot — beyond that, merge conflicts dominate.
slides.callout: Parallel agents are a force multiplier. Coordination overhead is the only limiting factor.

slides.heading: The Virtuous Cycle
slides.subheading: Plan → Prompt → Verify → Merge. Never skip a step.
points: Plan: generate a detailed execution strategy before any code is written.
Prompt: execute the plan with a scoped, context-rich agent prompt — one task at a time.
Verify: run automated tests, review the diff, and validate against the original spec.
Merge: only after verification passes — commit, tag, and document what was built.
The cycle is the product. Discipline in the loop compounds into quality at scale.
slides.callout: Skipping verification to ship faster is borrowing speed from tomorrow's debugging session.

slides.heading: The Local Engine
slides.subheading: Privacy, speed, and cost control — on your own hardware.
points: Local LLMs: run models like Llama 3, Mistral, and Phi-3 entirely on your own machine.
llmfit: one command to benchmark your hardware and find the optimal model-to-VRAM ratio.
Privacy advantage: sensitive codebases never leave your machine — zero data exposure risk.
Speed advantage: no network latency, no rate limits, no API costs for high-frequency tasks.
The sweet spot: a 7B–13B quantized model handles 80% of daily coding tasks locally.
slides.callout: A $2,000 GPU pays for itself in API costs within 6 months of serious development use.

slides.heading: The Toolbox
slides.subheading: Stand on the shoulders of the community — don't reinvent what already exists.
points: skills.sh: pre-built agent skill library — add capabilities to your agent in one line.
Awesome-Copilot: curated best practices for GitHub Copilot workflows and configurations.
Souls.directory: advanced Claude and agent persona prompts built by the community.
app.aitmpl.com/agents: production-ready agent templates for common project archetypes.
Context7 MCP: real-time, version-accurate documentation for any library your agent uses.
slides.callout: The best developers in the AI era are curators and connectors, not just creators.

slides.heading: Architect, Not Coder
slides.subheading: Your role has changed. Your identity needs to catch up.
points: You are a Product Owner now: you define intent, review output, and own the consequences.
Cognitive Offloading: use AI for grunt work — save your brain for business logic and judgment.
Critical Warning: if you can't debug manually, you will be locked out of your own production.
The new skill stack: system design, product thinking, prompt architecture, and QA mindset.
The identity shift: from someone who writes code to someone who governs code generation.
slides.callout: An architect who can't read blueprints is not an architect. Stay close to the code.

slides.heading: Continuous Learning
slides.subheading: The AI tooling landscape moves faster than any documentation can keep up with.
points: YouTube: search for your specific agent tool + workflow — community tutorials lead official docs.
Free certifications: Anthropic and Google both offer free AI developer courses — take them.
TLDR AI newsletter: curated daily digest of what actually matters in the AI ecosystem.
Fireship.io: 100-second technical breakdowns — the fastest way to evaluate new tools.
Reddit communities: r/LocalLLaMA, r/ClaudeAI, r/aipromptprogramming for real practitioner insight.
slides.callout: Spend 30 minutes a day learning the tools. The compounding knowledge advantage is enormous.

slides.heading: Thank you
slides.subheading: Your time to ask questions, share feedback, and connect.
points: You came here as a developer. You're leaving as an architect.
The tools exist. The frameworks are mature. The only barrier is your willingness to adapt.
slides.callout: The best time to start building with AI agents was last year. The second best time is right now.
